reasoning: The Federal Reserve has engaged in Quantitative Tightening (QT), steadily reducing its balance sheet over the past couple of years, creating a negative baseline trend. However, in 2024 and early 2025, the FOMC dramatically slowed the pace of this runoff, reducing redemption caps to minimal levels to smooth the end of the QT program. Analysts and Fed watchers expect QT to conclude fully in Q2/Q3 2025. Once QT is paused, organic growth in currency demand will likely cause the balance sheet to expand again. Additionally, balance sheet operations carry massive asymmetric upside tail risk — in the event of any unforeseen liquidity needs (like the SVB bank failure in 2023), the Fed can drastically expand its assets overnight. In the very short term (e.g., 1-4 weeks), the ongoing tapered runoff makes a decline slightly more likely than an increase, keeping odds below 0.5 (though short-term volatility and quarter-end repo operations provide a ~35-40% chance of a bump). Over a longer horizon (3-6 months), the expected end of QT, organic balance sheet growth, and upside tail risks significantly widen uncertainty, pushing the probability of an increase closer to an even 50/50, directly aligning with the instruction that uncertainty should increase and probabilities for distant dates should approach 0.5.
